notes.anchor: the hospitality of our friend Lucullus
notes.note: The host is the younger Lucullus, son of the great L. Licinius Lucullus (cos. 74) and inheritor of the famous Lucullan luxury-villa on the bay near Misenum; he is not separately registered, so the surface is left unbound. The reference is to the estate's celebrated hospitality, a fixture of the Campanian coast.

notes.anchor: on the sixth before the Ides
notes.note: A Roman inclusive-counting date: "the sixth day before the Ides of May," i.e. 10 May 44 BC. The Ides fell on the 15th in May, March, July, and October; dates are reckoned backward from it, counting both endpoints.

Buthrotum was a town in Epirus where Atticus held large estates; Caesar had assigned its territory to veteran colonists, and clearing that settlement ("the Buthrotum business") to protect Atticus's interests is a running thread of these post-Ides letters.

The mountainous region of central-southern Italy; Antony's journey there in May 44 BC was to canvass Caesar's settled veterans for armed support, hence Cicero's grim "of which you can guess what you can hope."

Lucius Antonius, youngest brother of Mark Antony and tribune of the plebs in 44 BC; his public speech (contio) struck Cicero as menacing, in contrast to the consul Dolabella's, which he found "brilliant."

notes.anchor: I am sorry Tertulla has miscarried
notes.note: Tertulla is Iunia Tertia, half-sister of Brutus and wife of Cassius; the following joke ("Cassii to plant as much as Bruti") puns grimly on her miscarriage and the wish for more men like the tyrannicides.

notes.anchor: About the queen --- and that ``Caesar'' of hers
notes.note: "The queen" is Cleopatra VII, who had been Caesar's guest in Rome and was now departing after the assassination; "that 'Caesar' of hers" is her son Caesarion, whom she styled as Caesar's child. Caesarion has no separate registry entry, so that surface is left unbound.

"De Quintis" most likely groups the troublesome nephew Quintus Cicero the younger (Antony's "right hand," see section 5) with his father; the surface is bound to the nephew, the active figure in these letters.

notes.anchor: It is Atilius, the harshest of poets
notes.note: Atilius was an early Roman dramatist (2nd century BC), proverbial for stiff, inelegant verse, whom Cicero quotes self-deprecatingly. He is not the same as the registry's Atilius the knight or Atilius Serranus, so the surface is left unbound.

notes.anchor: Quintus my nephew, as you write, is Antony's little right hand
notes.note: Cicero's nephew, Quintus Cicero the younger, had attached himself to Antony as a confidant ("dextella," diminutive of dextra, "right hand"); Cicero proposes cynically to exploit the connection.
